title: CODE OF HYGIENIC PRACTICE FOR COLLECTING, PROCESSING AND MARKETING OF NATURAL MINERAL WATERS

sections.section: CODE OF HYGIENIC PRACTICE FOR COLLECTING, PROCESSING AND MARKETING OF NATURAL MINERAL WATERS
sections.summary: Document title and code: Code of Hygienic Practice for Collecting, Processing and Marketing of Natural Mineral Waters (CXC 33-1985).

Introduces the Code as recommending hygienic practices for collecting, treating, bottling, packaging, storage, transport, distribution and sale of natural mineral waters for direct consumption, noting some bottled‑water hygiene controls cannot be used for natural mineral waters.

States the Code's objectives: identify requirements to ensure safe and suitable distribution of natural mineral waters, recommend using the General Principles of Food Hygiene approach, advise conducting specific hazard analysis (e.g., HACCP), and provide guidance with conditions specific to natural mineral waters.

sections.section: 2. SECTION II - SCOPE, USE AND DEFINITION
sections.summary: Section II sets out the Code's scope (applies to packaged natural mineral waters offered for sale as food), use (supplemental to and to be used with the General Principles of Food Hygiene, assuming their implementation and allowing regional modifications), and definitions; it also introduces a list of specific definitions relevant to natural mineral waters.
Specifies the Code applies to all packaged natural mineral waters offered for sale as food and does not apply to waters sold or used for other purposes.
States the Code is supplemental to the General Principles of Food Hygiene, assumes those principles are implemented, and may require modification for regional and hydro‑geological differences.

Declares that definitions from the General Principles of Food Hygiene apply and provides additional specific definitions (e.g., natural mineral waters as per CODEX STAN 108‑1981, adequate, aquifer, watershed, containers, groundwater, handling, packaging material, protection zone, pests, recharge, recharge rate, reservoir, safe yield, and spring).
Non‑substantive reference directing the reader to Section III of the General Principles of Food Hygiene.
Heading only: environmental hygiene — protection of aquifers; no substantive provisions in this entry.
Any spring, well or drilling intended for collection of natural mineral waters should be approved by the official authority having jurisdiction.

sections.section: 3.1.2  DETERMINATION OF THE GENESIS OF THE NATURAL MINERAL WATERS
sections.summary: Where methodologically possible, perform a precise analysis of the genesis, residence time in the ground, and the chemical and physical qualities of natural mineral waters.

Determine areas where natural mineral waters might be contaminated and, where indicated by hydrogeological conditions, establish one or more protection perimeters based on hydro‑geological studies by qualified experts that describe the watershed and include extraction point locations, aquifer extent/properties, watershed extent, natural protection, interacting surface waters, other abstractors, water chemistry/quality, recharge rate/safe yield and travel times to extraction points.

Take all precautions within protection perimeters to prevent contamination or other deterioration of natural mineral waters (including regulating waste disposal and risky substances and considering natural events), and evaluate threats by reviewing land ownership/use, contaminant data and applicable controls; define protection zones and monitoring programs based on that evaluation, extending at minimum to the producer's property and applying varying levels of protection by proximity and risk.

sections.section: ## 3.2 HYGIENIC EXTRACTION AND COLLECTION OF NATURAL MINERAL WATERS
sections.summary: Covers hygienic extraction and collection of natural mineral waters, addressing extraction methods to prevent ingress of extraneous water, protection of extraction areas, equipment/reservoir requirements, monitoring of physical/chemical/microbiological parameters and corrective actions.

Extraction must conform to hydrogeological conditions to prevent entry of any water other than the natural mineral water and be managed hygienically to protect against contamination from natural causes, human action, neglect or malice.

The immediate surroundings of springs and wells must be secured to prevent contaminant entry, be inaccessible to unauthorized persons, prohibit activities unrelated to water collection, and provide suitable paved access, drainage and signage as appropriate.
Extraction equipment and reservoirs must be designed and constructed to avoid contamination and preserve original water characteristics, with pipes, pumps and devices made of inert materials.

sections.section: 3.2.4 EXPLOITATION OF NATURAL MINERAL WATERS, MONITORING
sections.summary: Periodically examine extraction facilities, protection perimeters and water quality with documented automatic or manual measurements of appearance, odour, taste, flow rate, temperature, electrical conductivity, piezometric level, pH and relevant chemical parameters, perform microbiological monitoring at the source per the Table in Annex I at an appropriate frequency, and immediately record and implement corrective measures if criteria are not met.

sections.section: 3.3 HANDLING  AND  STORAGE  OF  NATURAL  MINERAL  WATERS  INTENDED  FOR PACKAGING
sections.summary: Heading only: handling and storage of natural mineral waters intended for packaging; no substantive provisions in this entry.

Methods and procedures for maintaining handling and storage facilities must be hygienic and meet the same servicing standards required for packaging or treatment to avoid health hazards or contamination of natural mineral waters.

sections.section: 3.3.2  STORAGE AT THE POINT OF EXTRACTION
sections.summary: Minimize the quantity and storage time of natural mineral waters at the point of extraction, enclose reservoirs to protect against contamination, filter or treat air entering reservoir headspace (air filters with pore size 0.45 µm or less), and design reservoirs to minimize time between extraction and packaging to avoid stagnation.

Piping and reservoirs from source to packaging must comply with official authority requirements and be made of approved inert food‑contact materials (e.g., ceramic, stainless steel) that prevent deterioration from water, handling, servicing or disinfection.

sections.section: 3.4 CLEANING, MAINTENANCE AND PERSONNEL HYGIENE AT PRIMARY PRODUCTION
sections.summary: Manage, maintain, clean and disinfect the extraction and supply network to protect against chemical, physical and microbiological contamination with disinfection regimes suited to operational risk and develop a contingency plan with experts/authorities for exceptional events; reservoirs must be cleaned, disinfected if necessary, and kept in good condition to avoid contamination or alteration of original water characteristics.

sections.section: 4. SECTION IV - ESTABLISHMENT: DESIGN AND FACILITIES
sections.summary: Non‑substantive reference directing the reader to Section IV of the General Principles of Food Hygiene.
Non‑substantive reference: consult the General Principles of Food Hygiene for location requirements.

Filling equipment (rinser, filler, capper) must be protected by positive‑pressure filtered or sterile air (cabinet or room) and open‑container activities should be confined to rinsing, filling and capping; operations that generate suspended particles (labelling, coding, shrink‑wrapping) should be excluded from filling areas and in‑room labelling machines require effective exhaust to prevent taste/odour changes.

sections.section: 37. The filling equipment (rinser, filler, capper) should be protected by a cabinet under positive pressure filtered air or in a room under sterile air filtration with positive pressure. It is advised to restrict operations in this  particular  area  to  a  minimum  by  confining  it  to the  open  container  activities  of  rinsing,  filling  and capping.
sections.summary: Filling equipment must be protected by a positive‑pressure filtered‑air cabinet or sterile room and operations in that area should be limited to rinsing, filling and capping.

Guidance on equipment contacting water (e.g., pumps, pipes, filling equipment): select water-contact materials carefully because water is a solvent; food-grade stainless steel is preferred and alternatives must be inert and approved for food contact, and lubricants must be suitable for food use while being prevented from contacting natural mineral water.

sections.section: 39. As water is one of nature's most effective solvents, care should be taken when selecting water contact materials. This should include the materials used in the manufacture of pumps, pipes, filling equipment, etc.
sections.summary: Advises careful selection of water-contact materials for equipment such as pumps, pipes and filling equipment because water is a strong solvent.

sections.section: 41. It is essential to verify that lubricants used are suitable for food use. However, care should be taken to avoid lubricants from coming into contact with natural mineral water.
sections.summary: Requires verification that lubricants are suitable for food use but cautions that lubricants should not come into contact with natural mineral water.
Section heading only (Facilities) with no substantive provisions in this entry.

Natural mineral water, potable and non-potable water lines should be completely separated (preferably visually differentiated, e.g., by colour), and steam used on surfaces in direct contact with natural mineral waters must contain no substances hazardous to health or that could contaminate the water.

Pipes, drains, sewage systems and waste bins within the protection perimeter must be designed and maintained to avoid contaminating aquifers; rejected containers (especially branded ones) must be prevented from unauthorized reuse and stored securely until disfigurement, destruction or authorized collection.
Non-substantive reference: cleaning requirements are covered by the General Principles of Food Hygiene.

Raw materials (e.g., CO2) and processing materials (e.g., filtration media) should be purchased from approved suppliers to agreed specifications, and steps should be taken to ensure CO2 does not introduce sensorial or microbiological contamination to the final product, containers or closures.

Containers must be stored to prevent contamination from volatile compounds, airborne contaminants, pests and malicious acts, and packaging materials must be kept dry and protected from heat, dust, pests and chemicals; use of recycled plastic packaging materials requires authorization by the official competent authority.
Non-substantive reference: water requirements are covered in Section 5.5.1 of the General Principles of Food Hygiene.
Non-substantive reference: management and supervision requirements are covered by the General Principles of Food Hygiene.
Non-substantive reference: documentation and records requirements are covered by the General Principles of Food Hygiene.
Non-substantive reference: recall procedure requirements are covered by the General Principles of Food Hygiene.

sections.section: 6. SECTION VI - ESTABLISHMENT: MAINTENANCE AND SANITATION
sections.summary: Section heading only referring readers to Section VI of the General Principles of Food Hygiene for maintenance and sanitation.

Require precautions to prevent contamination of natural mineral waters during cleaning/disinfection: use detergents/disinfectants suitable for purpose and acceptable to the competent authority, remove residues by thorough rinsing with potable or preferably natural mineral water, and use odour-free cleaning products; for dedicated packaging lines consider cold cleaning/disinfection and regular CIP/COP ensuring agents penetrate product flow and cover operational surfaces, while painting should be done outside production using low-odour, food-grade paints (preferably with mould inhibitor).
Non-substantive reference: cleaning programs are covered by the General Principles of Food Hygiene.

Refers to the General Principles of Food Hygiene and adds that toxic baits must not be used for internal pest control; insect-stunning devices must be located to prevent stunned insects or fragments falling into open containers, glue-board monitors are recommended with sufficiently large trays, and such devices must be regularly maintained and cleaned.
Non-substantive reference: waste management requirements are covered by the General Principles of Food Hygiene.
Non-substantive reference: monitoring effectiveness requirements are covered by the General Principles of Food Hygiene.
Non-substantive reference: personal hygiene requirements are covered by Section VII of the General Principles of Food Hygiene.

sections.section: 8. SECTION  VIII  -  TRANSPORTATION  AND  STORAGE  OF  PACKAGED  NATURAL MINERAL WATERS
sections.summary: Refers to General Principles of Food Hygiene and requires maintaining a minimum temperature to prevent freezing (which can cause container breakage/explosion and condensation-related label/packaging damage) and avoiding excessively high or low temperatures during storage and transport to prevent quality reduction including migration from packaging.

sections.section: 9. SECTION IX - PRODUCT INFORMATION AND CONSUMER AWARENESS
sections.summary: Non-substantive cross-reference directing readers to Section IX of the General Principles of Food Hygiene and Section VI of the Codex Standard for Natural Mineral Waters for product information and consumer awareness requirements.
Heading only for training; no substantive requirements or content provided in this section.

States the obligation that natural mineral waters must be microbiologically safe, links safety to hygiene controls from aquifer protection through packaging, and provides a table of microbiological criteria (including E. coli, total coliforms, enterococci, sulphite-reducing anaerobic spores, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, and aerobic mesophilic/heterotrophic counts) with sampling plan parameters (n, c, m) and ISO test methods for use by manufacturers and competent authorities at source, during production and for end-product verification.

Explains statistical performance assumptions for the sampling plan (log‑normal distribution, analytical SD = 0.25 log cfu/ml) and gives 95%‑confidence detection thresholds: (a) geometric mean ~2.3 cfu/l (~1 cfu/422 ml), (b) ~11.3 cfu/l (~1 cfu/88 ml), and (c) ~93 cfu/ml with a 100 cfu/ml exceedance criterion.

Specifies corrective actions when microbiological criteria are not met: prevent release of the affected water, determine and correct the root cause, and as appropriate review monitoring procedures and prerequisite programs.
Heading only for the rationale for chosen parameters; no substantive explanation provided in this text.
States that E. coli is considered one of the most suitable indicators of faecal contamination in natural mineral waters.

Explains that total coliforms may originate from faecal or environmental sources (soil, water, vegetation), are not normally present in natural mineral water sources, and are therefore an indicator of contamination at source or during packaging/process contact surfaces.
Heading only for enterococci; no descriptive content provided in this section.

Notes that spore-forming sulphite‑reducing anaerobes produce highly resistant spores, can originate from faecal contamination, and are used as indicators of faecal contamination due to their long survival in adverse environments.
Heading only for Pseudomonas aeruginosa; no substantive details provided in this section.

sections.section: Aerobic mesophilic count / heterotrophic plate count
sections.summary: States that the aerobic mesophilic / heterotrophic plate count reflects the natural flora and is used as a process management indicator, with limited increases from source to packaging being normal and larger increases indicating possible hygiene deterioration, stagnation or biofilm development.
